text:
EXAMINATION: Ga-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT, whole body with diagnostic CT chest/abdomen/pelvis

CLINICAL INDICATION: 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant. History of progressive disease on ADT plus abi; subsequently enrolled on Xtandi approximately 12 months prior to this examination. Recent clinical concern for biochemical progression with PSA rising from a nadir of 0.6 ng/mL to 8.3 ng/mL over three consecutive monthly measurements. New complaint of worsening low back pain and right hip pain. Restaging examination requested to assess extent and distribution of disease.

COMPARISON: Prior Ga-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT performed approximately 14 months prior (at time of castration-resistance establishment), and conventional bone scan with CT abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 6 months prior (on-Xtandi response assessment).

RADIOPHARMACEUTICAL: Ga-68 PSMA-11, 8.3 mCi administered intravenously. Imaging performed at 60 minutes post-injection. CT acquired with intravenous contrast (iohexol) for the diagnostic phase over chest, abdomen, and pelvis.

TECHNIQUE: Whole-body PET acquisition from vertex to mid-thigh in 7 bed positions. CT attenuation correction map generated. Diagnostic CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed in the portal venous phase. Maximum intensity projections (MIP) and fused PET/CT images reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. SUV measurements obtained using lean body mass correction (SUVmax reported).

--- FINDINGS ---

HEAD AND NECK:
No PSMA-avid foci identified in the calvarium, cervical spine, or soft tissues of the head and neck. No pathologically enlarged cervical or supraclavicular lymph nodes on CT. No suspicious intracranial lesions.

CHEST:
Lungs: No pulmonary nodules or masses identified. Lung parenchyma is clear bilaterally without consolidation, effusion, or interstitial abnormality. The visualized airways are patent.
Mediastinum: Mild mediastinal fat deposition. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No PSMA-avid mediastinal or hilar foci.
Pleura: No pleural effusions. No pleural thickening.
Cardiac: The heart is normal in size with no pericardial effusion. Mild coronary artery calcification noted, consistent with patient's known cardiovascular risk factors.
Chest wall and thoracic skeleton: Multiple foci of abnormal PSMA uptake and corresponding CT abnormalities identified as detailed below under osseous findings.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No hepatic lesions identified. No PSMA-avid foci within the hepatic parenchyma. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. Biliary ducts are not dilated.
Spleen: Normal in size and appearance. No focal splenic lesions.
Pancreas: The pancreas demonstrates normal attenuation throughout without focal mass, ductal dilation, or peripancreatic abnormality.
Adrenal glands: No adrenal enlargement or nodularity.
Kidneys/Ureters: Normal bilateral renal parenchyma and collecting systems. No hydronephrosis. Physiologic PSMA activity is noted in the kidneys bilaterally, as expected.
Bladder: The urinary bladder contains moderate urine and excreted radiotracer. No mural thickening or filling defect. Note is made of post-treatment changes in the prostate bed region consistent with prior radiation or hormonal therapy effect, with residual prostate gland appearing small and atrophic.
Bowel: No bowel obstruction, wall thickening, or mesenteric abnormality.

PELVIS / LYMPH NODES:
Right obturator lymph node: Markedly enlarged compared with prior examinations, now measuring 2.1 cm in short-axis diameter (previously 0.9 cm on CT six months prior; 1.4 cm on prior PSMA PET at time of castration-resistance diagnosis). The node demonstrates intense PSMA avidity with SUVmax of 18.7. This represents clear nodal progression.
Left pelvis: No new or enlarged pelvic lymph nodes identified on the left side. No left iliac chain adenopathy.
Retroperitoneal lymph nodes: No retroperitoneal or paraaortic lymphadenopathy. No PSMA-avid foci in the retroperitoneum.
Iliac lymph nodes: No external or internal iliac lymphadenopathy beyond the right obturator node described above. No common iliac nodal enlargement.
Inguinal lymph nodes: No enlarged or PSMA-avid inguinal lymph nodes bilaterally.

OSSEOUS FINDINGS — DETAILED:
There has been substantial worsening of the osseous disease burden compared to all prior imaging, with multiple new sites of involvement as detailed below.

Spine:
— L3 and L4 vertebral bodies: Previously identified lesions demonstrate markedly increased PSMA avidity (SUVmax L3: 14.2, up from 9.1 on prior PSMA PET; SUVmax L4: 12.8, up from 7.6). CT shows mixed sclerotic and lytic components; the lytic component appears expanded compared to the prior sclerotic-predominant appearance, suggesting active disease within previously treated foci. No cortical destruction or epidural extension identified.
— T10 vertebral body: Previously identified lesion with increased PSMA avidity (SUVmax 11.4, up from 8.3 on prior PSMA PET). CT demonstrates persistent sclerotic density with subtle increase in size.
— L1 and L2 vertebral bodies: NEW foci of PSMA avidity at both L1 (SUVmax 9.6) and L2 (SUVmax 8.1), not present on prior imaging. CT demonstrates subtle new mixed density changes within both vertebral bodies without significant cortical involvement. These represent new metastatic deposits.
— Thoracic spine (T5, T7): Two additional new foci of mild-to-moderate PSMA uptake identified in mid-thoracic spine (SUVmax 6.4 and 5.9 respectively). CT correlation shows very subtle sclerotic foci, compatible with early osseous involvement.

Ribs:
— Right posterior 7th rib: Previously identified lesion demonstrates increased PSMA avidity (SUVmax 10.1, up from 7.4). CT demonstrates a sclerotic lesion with mild interval increase in density.
— Right posterior 9th rib: NEW PSMA-avid focus (SUVmax 7.8). CT shows a subtle new area of sclerotic density along the posterior cortex consistent with a new metastatic deposit.
— Left posterior 9th rib: Previously identified lesion with mildly increased PSMA avidity (SUVmax 8.4, up from 6.1). CT demonstrates stable-appearing sclerotic lesion.
— Left anterior 4th rib: NEW PSMA-avid focus (SUVmax 6.9). CT demonstrates a subtle new area of cortical thickening and sclerosis.
— Right anterior 6th rib: NEW PSMA-avid focus (SUVmax 7.2). CT shows subtle new sclerotic change.
— Right posterior 11th rib (costovertebral junction): NEW small PSMA-avid focus (SUVmax 5.7). CT demonstrates a subtle new sclerotic lesion at the costovertebral junction.
— Left 6th rib anterolateral: NEW PSMA-avid focus (SUVmax 6.3). CT demonstrates subtle new sclerotic density.
In summary, at least 5 new rib lesions are identified in addition to previously known rib metastases with increasing disease activity.

Sternum:
— Sternal body (mid-sternum): NEW discrete focus of intense PSMA avidity (SUVmax 11.8). CT demonstrates a new focal sclerotic lesion in the sternal body, not present on prior imaging. This represents new metastatic involvement of the sternum.

Pelvis:
— Right sacral ala: Previously identified lesion demonstrates modestly increased PSMA avidity (SUVmax 9.3, up from 7.2). CT demonstrates a persistent but slightly expanded sclerotic lesion.
— Right iliac wing: Previously identified lesion with increased avidity (SUVmax 8.6). CT shows mixed sclerotic and lytic changes with slight interval increase in size.
— Bilateral iliac crests: NEW bilateral PSMA-avid foci are identified at both iliac crests — right iliac crest (SUVmax 10.4) and left iliac crest (SUVmax 9.1). CT demonstrates new mixed density sclerotic/lytic lesions bilaterally at the iliac crest margins. These are new since prior imaging and represent new sites of metastatic disease.

Femur / Proximal long bones:
— Right femoral head and neck: Previously identified lesion — notably, there has been marked interval enlargement and dramatically increased PSMA avidity in this region (SUVmax 16.3, up from 8.8 on prior PSMA PET). CT demonstrates expansion of the right femoral neck lesion with mixed lytic and sclerotic morphology. The cortex of the femoral neck appears thinned along the superior aspect. The lesion measures approximately 3.4 cm in greatest dimension. This represents marked progression of a previously identified lesion. Orthopedic correlation may be considered given the degree of cortical thinning, though frank pathologic fracture is not identified on this examination.
— Left femur: No PSMA-avid foci in the left femur.
— Bilateral humeri, tibiae, and fibulae (partially imaged): No additional PSMA-avid foci identified in the partially visualized appendicular skeleton.

VISCERAL METASTASES:
No PSMA-avid hepatic lesions. No PSMA-avid pulmonary lesions. No adrenal metastases. No soft tissue masses identified.

PSMA-NEGATIVE LESIONS:
No CT-identified soft tissue lesions or lymph nodes that are PSMA-negative and of indeterminate nature. No liver lesions of concern on contrast-enhanced CT. No CT-identified lesions felt to be discordant from PSMA uptake pattern.

--- IMPRESSION ---

1. SUBSTANTIAL DISEASE PROGRESSION compared to all prior imaging. There has been dramatic worsening of the osseous metastatic burden with multiple new sites of PSMA-avid skeletal disease.

2. NEW OSSEOUS METASTASES (since prior imaging): New lesions identified at L1 vertebral body, L2 vertebral body, T5 vertebral body, T7 vertebral body, the sternal body, bilateral iliac crests, right anterior 6th rib, right posterior 9th rib, right posterior 11th rib, left anterior 4th rib, and left anterolateral 6th rib — totaling at minimum 11 new osseous foci not present on prior imaging.

3. PROGRESSION OF KNOWN OSSEOUS LESIONS: Previously identified lesions at L3, L4, T10, right 7th rib, left 9th rib, right sacral ala, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck/head all demonstrate increased PSMA avidity and/or interval CT changes reflecting active disease progression. The right femoral neck lesion is particularly notable for marked enlargement and cortical thinning; orthopedic surgery awareness is suggested given potential structural implications.

4. NODAL PROGRESSION: The right obturator lymph node has enlarged substantially to 2.1 cm with markedly increased PSMA avidity, consistent with progressive nodal metastatic disease.

5. NO VISCERAL METASTASES IDENTIFIED: No hepatic, pulmonary, adrenal, or soft tissue metastatic disease is identified on this examination. PSMA-negative lesion pattern is not present.

6. Overall findings are consistent with extensive and progressive bony and nodal metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, having progressed through enza. The degree of disease spread represents a significant and clinically important interval change from prior imaging.
